I probably should’ve written this years ago.
Over the years, I’ve been on both sides of the technical interview table. I’ve done the LeetCode grind myself. I’ve spent nights preparing for algorithm interviews, memorizing patterns, and practicing problems before interviews at large tech companies.
Later, as my career progressed and I moved into AI engineering, I started interviewing candidates as well. After dozens of interviews, something became increasingly obvious:
LeetCode performance is a surprisingly weak predictor of how someone will actually perform as an engineer.
And with the rise of AI, its relevance is shrinking even further.

When LeetCode Actually Made Sense
To be fair, LeetCode-style questions weren’t always unreasonable.
At one point, they served a practical purpose. Large tech companies needed a scalable way to screen thousands of applicants. Algorithmic problems were standardized, language-agnostic, and relatively difficult to fake.
If someone could derive a dynamic programming recurrence, reason about time complexity, and implement an efficient solution under pressure, it suggested a strong foundation in computer science.
When the problems were unfamiliar and the patterns weren’t widely documented, solving them required genuine problem-solving ability.
But that environment doesn’t exist anymore.

The Preparation Arms Race
Over time, the entire ecosystem around these interviews changed.
Once a test becomes predictable, it stops measuring ability and starts measuring preparation.
Today, most serious candidates don’t approach LeetCode problems fresh. They spend months memorizing patterns:

• Sliding window

• Two pointers

• Binary search

• Monotonic stacks

• Graph traversal algorithms

• Dynamic programming templates
Entire websites, courses, and preparation guides now exist specifically to train people for these patterns.
When I interview candidates today, I often see the same thing: someone recognizes the problem pattern within seconds and jumps straight to the “correct” solution.
But it’s not necessarily because they solved the problem from first principles. It’s because they’ve seen a nearly identical problem before.
At that point, the interview is no longer testing engineering ability.
It’s testing how much someone has practiced the interview.

AI Has Broken the Model Completely
Then AI entered the picture.
As someone working in AI, I interact with these models every day. And the reality is simple: modern models can solve a large portion of LeetCode problems almost instantly.
Give an LLM a typical algorithm question and it will often produce a correct solution in seconds.
The barrier to generating the algorithm itself has collapsed, which raises an uncomfortable question: What exactly are we measuring when we ask a candidate to derive the same solution on a whiteboard?
We’re not testing real-world engineering ability. We’re testing whether someone can temporarily operate without tools.
But software engineers don’t work without tools. They work with IDEs, documentation, Stack Overflow, and now increasingly, AI assistants.
Evaluating engineers in a tool-free environment increasingly feels like testing pilots on how well they can fly without instruments.

What Actually Predicts Engineering Ability
After interviewing many candidates and working with engineers across multiple teams, a pattern becomes clear.
The skills that actually matter day-to-day rarely resemble LeetCode problems.
Real engineering work looks more like this:

• Understanding a large, messy existing codebase

• Debugging unexpected behavior in production

• Designing maintainable systems

• Reviewing other people’s code

• Identifying edge cases before they cause outages

• Communicating trade-offs with other engineers
None of these look like reversing a linked list or implementing a trie in 30 minutes.
In fact, some of the best engineers I’ve worked with weren’t particularly strong at algorithm interviews. But they were excellent at system thinking, debugging complex issues, and building reliable software.
Those are the skills that actually ship products.

Interviews Are Already Changing
One thing I’ve noticed over the years is that interviews themselves are slowly evolving.
More teams are starting to incorporate practical evaluations:

• More emphasis on system design interviews, even at more junior levels

• Code review exercises

• Debugging tasks

• Take-home projects

• Pair-programming sessions
These formats are far from perfect, but they test something closer to real engineering work.
Instead of asking “Can this person recall an algorithm pattern?”, the question becomes:
Can this person work effectively on a real system with other engineers?
That’s a much more meaningful signal.

The Shift in the AI Era
AI is also shifting what engineering work actually looks like.
Writing syntactically correct code is becoming easier. AI tools can generate boilerplate, suggest implementations, and even produce full working functions and solutions.
But the hardest parts of engineering remain deeply human:

• Defining the right problem

• Structuring systems correctly

• Evaluating design trade-offs

• Reviewing and validating generated code

• Thinking about long-term maintainability
In other words, the bottleneck is moving higher up the abstraction stack.
Engineers are becoming less like typists and more like architects, reviewers, and decision-makers.
Ironically, those are exactly the skills that LeetCode interviews barely measure.

The Real Cost of the Grind
The biggest problem with the LeetCode obsession isn’t just that it’s imperfect.
It’s that it consumes an enormous amount of time that could be spent learning more valuable skills.
I’ve seen candidates spend hundreds of hours grinding algorithm problems. Some build elaborate study schedules and track dozens of problem patterns.
But imagine if even half of that time were spent:

• Building real systems

• Contributing to open-source projects

• Reading production codebases

• Learning system design

• Debugging complex software
Those experiences compound over time. Memorized interview patterns do not.

What I Tell Younger Engineers
If you’re early in your career, you probably can’t ignore LeetCode completely. Some companies still rely heavily on it.
But it shouldn’t become your entire definition of engineering ability.
Learn the fundamentals — data structures, complexity analysis, algorithmic thinking. Those are important.
But don’t confuse interview preparation with engineering mastery.
Spend most of your time building things, understanding systems, and learning how real software behaves under real constraints.
Because as AI continues to reshape how code gets written, the engineers who thrive will not be the fastest puzzle solvers.
They will be the ones who understand what should be built, why it should be built, and whether it actually works.
